stem: PALM-COEIN: what does the 'O' stand for?

choices.feedback: O stands for Ovulatory dysfunction.

Correct! PALM-COEIN: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy (structural causes) + Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified (non-structural). Ovulatory dysfunction (anovulation) is one of the most common causes of AUB, especially PCOS.
Ovarian cysts are not part of PALM-COEIN.
Malignancy is represented by M, not O.

explanation: In the PALM-COEIN classification of abnormal uterine bleeding (FIGO 2011), O = Ovulatory dysfunction. The full classification: PALM (structural causes) = Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia; COEIN (non-structural causes) = Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not otherwise classified. Ovulatory dysfunction is the most common cause of AUB in adolescents and perimenopausal women. Obstruction and oncologic emergency are not part of this classification.

stem: First test in a reproductive-age woman with AUB?

choices.feedback: CT is not first-line for AUB evaluation.
Biopsy is indicated if >45 or with risk factors, but pregnancy test comes first.

Correct! ALWAYS rule out pregnancy first in reproductive-age AUB. Ectopic pregnancy, threatened/inevitable abortion, and molar pregnancy can all present with abnormal bleeding. After negative β-hCG: CBC, TSH, coagulation studies (if indicated), pelvic US.
US is important but not first. Rule out pregnancy first.

explanation: A pregnancy test (urine or serum β-hCG) is always the first test in any reproductive-age woman with abnormal uterine bleeding. Pregnancy and its complications (ectopic pregnancy, threatened abortion, gestational trophoblastic disease) must be excluded before further workup or treatment. CT pelvis involves radiation and is not first-line. Endometrial biopsy is indicated for women >45 or with risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia/cancer. Pelvic ultrasound is an important second step but pregnancy must be excluded first.

stem: Most effective MEDICAL treatment for menorrhagia?

choices.feedback: OCPs reduce bleeding ~40% but are not the most effective medical option.

Correct! LNG-IUD reduces menstrual blood loss by >90%, superior to all other medical therapies. Causes endometrial atrophy. Also treats endometrial hyperplasia without atypia. Comparable to hysterectomy for quality of life improvement while preserving fertility. First-line medical therapy for heavy menstrual bleeding.
Tranexamic acid reduces bleeding ~40% — effective but less than LNG-IUD.
Iron treats the resulting anemia but doesn't reduce bleeding.

explanation: The levonorgestrel intrauterine device (LNG-IUD, e.g., Mirena) is the most effective medical treatment for menorrhagia (heavy menstrual bleeding). It reduces menstrual blood loss by >90% through local progestogenic effects on the endometrium (decidualization and atrophy). It is superior to combined OCPs, tranexamic acid, and oral progestins in randomized trials. It also serves as contraception and endometrial protection. Iron supplementation treats resulting anemia but does not reduce bleeding.

stem: #1 cause of anovulatory AUB in reproductive-age women?

choices.feedback: Correct! PCOS is the most common cause of anovulatory bleeding in reproductive-age women. Features: irregular periods, hyperandrogenism (acne, hirsutism), polycystic ovaries on US. Rotterdam criteria: 2 of 3 (oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries). Associated with insulin resistance, obesity, type 2 DM risk.
Endometriosis causes dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain, not typically anovulatory bleeding.
Thyroid dysfunction causes AUB but is not the #1 cause of anovulation.
Cervical cancer causes postcoital or intermenstrual bleeding, not anovulation.

explanation: PCOS is the most common cause of anovulatory AUB in reproductive-age women, affecting 6-12% of this population. Chronic anovulation leads to unopposed estrogen stimulation of the endometrium, resulting in irregular and often heavy bleeding. PCOS diagnosis requires 2 of 3 Rotterdam criteria: oligo/anovulation, clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound. Endometriosis causes dysmenorrhea and chronic pelvic pain. Hypothyroidism can cause AUB but is less common than PCOS. Cervical cancer causes intermenstrual/postcoital bleeding.

stem: Submucosal fibroids cause the MOST bleeding because:

choices.feedback: Size doesn't determine which type bleeds most.

choices.text: They have the highest malignant potential
choices.feedback: Malignant transformation is extremely rare for all fibroid types.
Fibroids are estrogen-responsive but don't produce estrogen.

Correct! Submucosal fibroids project into the uterine cavity, distorting the endometrium and increasing endometrial surface area → heavier menstrual bleeding. Subserosal fibroids (projecting outward) cause bulk symptoms (pressure, urinary frequency) but less bleeding. Intramural (within the wall) cause intermediate symptoms.

explanation: Submucosal fibroids (FIGO type 0-2) cause the most significant bleeding because they protrude into and distort the endometrial cavity, increasing endometrial surface area, disrupting normal endometrial vasculature, and interfering with normal uterine contractility that controls menstrual bleeding. They are not necessarily the largest fibroids — intramural and subserosal fibroids can be much larger. Fibroids (leiomyomas) are benign and have very low malignant potential. Fibroids are estrogen/progesterone-responsive but do not produce estrogen.

stem: Adolescent with heavy menstrual bleeding since menarche. Screen for:

choices.feedback: Ovarian cancer is extremely rare in adolescents.
Endometrial cancer is rare in adolescents.
BRCA screening is not indicated for menorrhagia.

Correct! ~20% of adolescents hospitalized for menorrhagia have an underlying bleeding disorder, most commonly von Willebrand disease (vWD — type 1 most common). Screen: CBC, PT/PTT, vWF antigen, vWF activity (ristocetin cofactor), factor VIII. Heavy periods since menarche = red flag for coagulopathy.

explanation: Von Willebrand disease (vWD) should be screened for in any adolescent presenting with heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) since menarche, as vWD is the most common inherited bleeding disorder (affecting ~1% of the population) and HMB is often its presenting symptom in females. ACOG recommends screening for bleeding disorders in adolescents with HMB. Initial testing includes vWF antigen, vWF activity (ristocetin cofactor), and factor VIII level. Ovarian and endometrial cancers are extremely rare in adolescents. BRCA testing is not indicated for HMB.

stem: Postmenopausal AUB — #1 cause?

choices.feedback: Fibroids shrink after menopause (estrogen-dependent). Rarely cause postmenopausal bleeding.
Cancer is the most concerning cause but not the most common.
PCOS is a reproductive-age diagnosis. Not relevant postmenopausally.

Correct! Atrophy is the #1 cause of postmenopausal bleeding (60-80%). Due to estrogen deficiency. However, endometrial cancer must always be excluded. Workup: TVUS → if endometrial thickness >4mm → endometrial biopsy. ~10% of postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer.

explanation: Endometrial and vaginal atrophy is the most common cause of postmenopausal bleeding (PMB), accounting for approximately 60-80% of cases. Estrogen deficiency after menopause leads to thin, fragile endometrial and vaginal tissue prone to bleeding. However, endometrial cancer must always be excluded because it accounts for ~10% of PMB cases. Any postmenopausal bleeding warrants transvaginal ultrasound (endometrial thickness) and possible endometrial biopsy. Fibroids and PCOS are primarily reproductive-age conditions.

stem: Rapidly growing fibroid in a postmenopausal woman raises concern for:

choices.feedback: Correct! Fibroids are estrogen-dependent and should not grow after menopause. Rapid growth postmenopausally raises concern for leiomyosarcoma (uterine sarcoma). Imaging cannot reliably distinguish benign from malignant. Surgical excision recommended. Overall malignant transformation of fibroids is rare (<1%) but must be considered in this scenario.
Torsion presents acutely with pain, not as gradual growth.
Fibroids should SHRINK after menopause due to estrogen decline. Growth is concerning.
Endometriosis also regresses postmenopausally.

explanation: A rapidly growing uterine mass in a postmenopausal woman raises concern for leiomyosarcoma, a rare but aggressive uterine malignancy. Normal fibroids should not grow after menopause (they are estrogen-dependent and typically shrink). Rapid growth, especially without exogenous estrogen, is a red flag requiring tissue sampling. MRI with gadolinium can help differentiate leiomyosarcoma from benign fibroids. Ovarian torsion presents with acute unilateral pelvic pain. Normal fibroid growth in menopause is unexpected. Endometriosis regresses after menopause.

stem: Most effective reversible contraception?

choices.feedback: OCPs have 7% typical-use failure rate. LARCs are superior.
DMPA has 4-6% typical-use failure rate.

Correct! Long-acting reversible contraceptives: subdermal implant (<0.1% failure) and IUDs (copper <1%, LNG-IUD <1%). 'Set and forget' — no adherence issues. ACOG recommends as first-line for all women including nulliparous and adolescents.
Condoms have ~13% typical-use failure. Important for STI prevention but less effective for pregnancy prevention alone.

explanation: Long-acting reversible contraceptives (LARCs) — IUDs (hormonal and copper) and the etonogestrel subdermal implant — are the most effective reversible methods with <1% failure rate, equivalent to sterilization. Their efficacy is user-independent (no daily adherence required). Combined OCPs have a typical-use failure rate of ~7%. DMPA injection has ~4% typical-use failure. Male condoms have ~13% typical-use failure. ACOG recommends LARCs as first-line contraception for most women.

stem: Estrogen-containing contraceptives are CONTRAINDICATED in migraine with aura because:

choices.feedback: The risk is inherent to the combination of aura + estrogen, not a drug interaction.

choices.text: Estrogen causes seizures in migraine patients
choices.feedback: Estrogen does not cause seizures.

Correct! Migraine with aura is an independent stroke risk factor. Adding estrogen (which increases thrombotic risk) creates unacceptable stroke risk. Migraine WITHOUT aura = combined contraceptives are acceptable. Migraine WITH aura = progestin-only methods only. This is one of the most commonly tested contraindications.
The concern is not headache frequency.

explanation: Estrogen-containing contraceptives (combined OCPs, patch, ring) are WHO Category 4 contraindicated in migraine with aura due to a 2-4 fold increased risk of ischemic stroke. Migraine with aura is an independent stroke risk factor, and estrogen further increases thrombotic risk through prothrombotic effects on coagulation factors. Progestin-only methods (POP, DMPA, implant, hormonal IUD) are safe alternatives. Aura medications do not specifically interact with estrogen. Estrogen does not cause seizures. While estrogen can affect headache patterns, stroke risk is the critical contraindication.

stem: Most effective emergency contraception?

choices.feedback: Correct! Copper IUD is >99% effective as emergency contraception when placed within 5 days of unprotected intercourse. Also serves as ongoing contraception for 10-12 years. Ulipristal (Ella) is second-most effective (~98%, up to 5 days). Plan B is least effective of the three options.
Yuzpe method is older and less effective with more side effects.
Mifepristone is used for medical abortion, not standard emergency contraception in the US.
Plan B is ~89% effective and decreases with time and weight.

explanation: The copper IUD is the most effective emergency contraception, with >99% efficacy when placed within 5 days of unprotected intercourse. It prevents fertilization through copper ion toxicity to sperm and also has post-fertilization effects. Additionally, it provides ongoing contraception for up to 10-12 years. The Yuzpe method (high-dose combined OCPs) is older and less effective with more side effects. Mifepristone is not standard EC in the US. Plan B (levonorgestrel 1.5 mg) is widely available but less effective, especially in women >75 kg.

stem: Which contraceptive method also protects against STIs?

choices.feedback: Correct! Condoms are the ONLY contraceptive method that provides STI protection (including HIV). Dual method use (condom + LARC or hormonal method) recommended for both pregnancy and STI prevention. Female condoms (internal condoms) also provide STI protection.
OCPs provide no STI protection.
DMPA prevents pregnancy only.
IUDs prevent pregnancy but not STIs.

explanation: Male and female (internal) condoms are the only contraceptive methods that also provide barrier protection against sexually transmitted infections, including HIV, chlamydia, gonorrhea, and syphilis. Male latex condoms reduce HIV transmission risk by approximately 80% with consistent use. All other methods (OCPs, DMPA, IUDs) provide no STI protection. Dual method use (condoms + hormonal method) is recommended for patients needing both pregnancy prevention and STI protection.

stem: A breastfeeding mother <6 weeks postpartum should AVOID:

choices.feedback: Progestin-only methods are safe and do not affect milk supply.

Correct! Estrogen can decrease breast milk supply, especially in early postpartum. Avoid combined hormonal contraceptives (OCP, patch, ring) until ≥6 weeks postpartum (some guidelines say ≥6 months). Progestin-only methods are safe: mini-pill, implant, LNG-IUD, DMPA.
Barrier methods are always safe.
Copper IUD is safe during breastfeeding (hormone-free).

explanation: Estrogen-containing contraceptives should be avoided in breastfeeding women <6 weeks postpartum because estrogen can suppress lactation by reducing milk production. Additionally, the early postpartum period carries elevated thromboembolism risk, which estrogen further increases. Progestin-only methods (POP, implant, DMPA, hormonal IUD) are safe during breastfeeding and do not significantly affect milk supply. Condoms and copper IUD are also safe. After 6 weeks, combined hormonal methods can be considered if breastfeeding is well established.

stem: Bilateral salpingectomy is increasingly preferred over tubal ligation because:

choices.feedback: Neither procedure is easily reversible. Bilateral salpingectomy is actually less reversible.
Both are permanent sterilization methods.

choices.text: It is less invasive than tubal ligation
choices.feedback: Salpingectomy is slightly more extensive surgically.

Correct! Growing evidence that high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma originates from the fallopian tube fimbriae. Bilateral salpingectomy removes this tissue, potentially reducing ovarian cancer risk by 40-65%. ACOG supports opportunistic salpingectomy when permanent sterilization is desired.

explanation: Bilateral salpingectomy (complete fallopian tube removal) is increasingly preferred over tubal ligation because it significantly reduces the risk of ovarian cancer. Current evidence suggests that many high-grade serous ovarian cancers originate from precursor lesions (serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma, STIC) in the fimbriated end of the fallopian tube. Salpingectomy is not easier to reverse — it is irreversible (unlike tubal ligation which has some reversal potential). It does not preserve more fertility options. The surgical complexity is similar to tubal ligation.

stem: Plan B (levonorgestrel) efficacy decreases with:

choices.feedback: Repeated use doesn't decrease efficacy.
Age is not the primary factor affecting efficacy.

choices.text: Body weight >75 kg and time delay
choices.feedback: Correct! Levonorgestrel efficacy decreases significantly with higher BMI (body weight >75 kg / BMI >25). Also less effective after 72h. For overweight patients or if >72h: ulipristal (Ella) or copper IUD are better options. Copper IUD is the most effective regardless of weight.
Most antibiotics don't interact with Plan B (rifampin is an exception).

explanation: Plan B (levonorgestrel 1.5 mg) efficacy is reduced in women weighing >75 kg (165 lbs), with significantly decreased effectiveness above 80 kg. Efficacy also declines with increasing time from unprotected intercourse — it is most effective within 12 hours and should be taken within 72 hours (some benefit up to 120 hours). For women >75 kg, ulipristal acetate (ella) or copper IUD are preferred alternatives. Previous Plan B use does not reduce its future efficacy. Age over 30 does not affect efficacy. Concurrent antibiotic use does not typically interact with levonorgestrel.

stem: Copper IUD mechanism of contraception:

choices.feedback: Cervical mucus thickening is a progestin effect. Copper IUD is hormone-free.
Copper IUD is hormone-free and does not affect ovulation.
The IUD doesn't physically block the cervix.

choices.text: Copper ions are toxic to sperm, creating an inflammatory intrauterine environment that prevents fertilization
choices.feedback: Correct! Copper ions create a sterile inflammatory reaction toxic to sperm and ova, preventing fertilization. Also alters endometrial lining to prevent implantation. No hormonal effects → no systemic side effects. Can increase menstrual bleeding/cramping. Effective for 10-12 years.

explanation: The copper IUD (Paragard) works through the toxic effects of copper ions on sperm, impairing sperm motility and viability. Copper also creates a sterile inflammatory reaction within the uterus that is spermicidal and prevents implantation. It is hormone-free and effective for up to 10-12 years. The copper IUD does not thicken cervical mucus (that is the mechanism of hormonal IUDs/progestin methods). It does not prevent ovulation. It does not mechanically block the cervical os — the device sits within the uterine cavity.

stem: Most common location for ectopic pregnancy?

choices.feedback: Abdominal pregnancy is extremely rare.

Correct! Ampulla (~70%), then isthmus (~12%), fimbria (~11%), interstitial/cornual (~2%). Ampullary ectopics are most common because fertilization normally occurs here — the embryo may implant before reaching the uterus if tubal transport is impaired.
Cervical ectopics are rare (<1%) but dangerous (massive hemorrhage).
Ovarian ectopics are rare (<3%).

explanation: The ampulla (distal third) of the fallopian tube is the most common location for ectopic pregnancy, accounting for approximately 70% of cases. The isthmus accounts for ~12%, fimbrial ~11%, and interstitial (cornual) ~2-3%. Ovarian, cervical, and abdominal (peritoneal) ectopics are rare. The ampulla is the most common site because it is where fertilization normally occurs, and tubal damage from prior infection, surgery, or endometriosis can impede the embryo's transit to the uterus.

stem: β-hCG above discriminatory zone + empty uterus on TVUS means:

choices.feedback: Above discriminatory zone, a normal IUP should be visible.
Miscarriage shows intrauterine contents (POC), not empty uterus with high β-hCG.

Correct! If β-hCG >1500-3500 mIU/mL and TVUS shows no IUP, presume ectopic. Further evaluation: adnexal mass? Free fluid? If β-hCG below discriminatory zone with empty uterus = pregnancy of unknown location → serial β-hCG q48h to determine trajectory.
Molar pregnancy shows intrauterine 'snowstorm' pattern, not empty uterus.

explanation: When β-hCG is above the discriminatory zone (typically 1500-2000 mIU/mL for TVUS) and no intrauterine pregnancy is visualized, ectopic pregnancy must be presumed until proven otherwise. This is a critical clinical decision point. An intrauterine pregnancy should be visible by TVUS at these levels. Very early normal pregnancy is unlikely above the discriminatory zone (by definition). Inevitable miscarriage would typically show intrauterine products. Molar pregnancy usually shows a 'snowstorm' pattern on ultrasound with disproportionately high β-hCG.

stem: Methotrexate for ectopic is appropriate when:

choices.text: Stable, unruptured, β-hCG <5000, no fetal cardiac activity
choices.feedback: Correct! MTX criteria: hemodynamically stable, unruptured, β-hCG <5000 (best results), no fetal cardiac activity on US, ectopic mass <3.5 cm, reliable patient for follow-up. Folic acid antagonist → inhibits trophoblastic proliferation. Monitor β-hCG to zero. Contraindicated: hepatic/renal disease, immunodeficiency, breastfeeding.

choices.text: Any ectopic regardless of size or stability
choices.feedback: MTX has specific criteria and contraindications.
Instability = ruptured ectopic → emergent surgery, not MTX.

choices.text: Only if β-hCG is declining spontaneously
choices.feedback: Declining β-hCG may qualify for expectant management instead of MTX.

explanation: Methotrexate (single-dose IM protocol) is appropriate for hemodynamically stable patients with an unruptured ectopic, β-hCG ideally <5000 mIU/mL (higher levels associated with treatment failure), no fetal cardiac activity on TVUS, and tubal mass <3.5-4 cm. Methotrexate inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, blocking rapidly dividing trophoblastic cells. It is not appropriate for any ectopic regardless of parameters. Hemodynamically unstable patients require emergent surgical intervention (salpingectomy or salpingostomy). Spontaneously declining β-hCG may be managed expectantly without methotrexate.

stem: Normal intrauterine pregnancy β-hCG doubling time?

choices.feedback: Correct! Normal early IUP: β-hCG doubles approximately every 48h. Abnormal rise (<35% increase in 48h) suggests ectopic or nonviable pregnancy. Declining β-hCG suggests miscarriage. Used for pregnancy of unknown location (PUL) to determine viability and location.

choices.text: β-hCG doesn't change in normal pregnancy
choices.feedback: β-hCG rises steadily in early pregnancy, peaking at 8-10 weeks.
Weekly is too slow for normal doubling.
24h is too fast for normal β-hCG doubling.

explanation: In a viable early intrauterine pregnancy, β-hCG approximately doubles every 48 hours (minimum 35% rise in 48 hours is considered normal). A slower-than-expected rise suggests ectopic pregnancy or nonviable intrauterine pregnancy, while a decline suggests pregnancy failure. Serial β-hCG monitoring is essential when the initial ultrasound is inconclusive. β-hCG levels plateau and begin declining around 10-12 weeks as the placenta takes over hormone production. Doubling does not occur every 24 hours (too fast) or every week (too slow).

stem: #1 risk factor for ectopic pregnancy?

choices.feedback: OCPs are protective against pregnancy and thus ectopic.
Smoking impairs tubal motility but is not the highest risk factor.
Correct! Prior ectopic = 10-25% recurrence risk (strongest risk factor). Other major risks: PID/STI (Chlamydia causes tubal scarring), prior tubal surgery, IVF, smoking, DES exposure, endometriosis.
Age mildly increases risk but is not the #1 factor.

explanation: Prior ectopic pregnancy is the strongest single risk factor for subsequent ectopic, with a recurrence rate of approximately 10-25%. Other major risk factors include prior tubal surgery, PID/salpingitis history, tubal pathology, IVF, smoking, and DES exposure. The common mechanism is tubal damage impairing embryo transport. OCP use actually decreases overall pregnancy risk (including ectopic). Smoking is a significant risk factor but not the #1. Advanced maternal age is a minor risk factor compared to prior ectopic.

stem: Rh-negative patient with ectopic pregnancy should receive:

choices.feedback: Folate prevents neural tube defects but isn't specifically indicated for ectopic.
Transfusion only if hemodynamically unstable from hemorrhage.
Rh status is always relevant in pregnancy-related bleeding.

choices.text: RhoGAM (anti-D immunoglobulin) to prevent Rh sensitization
choices.feedback: Correct! ALL Rh-negative women with any pregnancy event (ectopic, miscarriage, abortion, trauma, delivery) should receive RhoGAM within 72h to prevent Rh isoimmunization. Standard dose: IM. Prevents formation of anti-D antibodies that could cause hemolytic disease in future pregnancies.

explanation: All Rh-negative women with ectopic pregnancy (or any pregnancy event involving potential fetomaternal hemorrhage) should receive RhoGAM (anti-D immunoglobulin, 50 mcg for first trimester, 300 mcg after 12 weeks) to prevent Rh alloimmunization. Without RhoGAM, an Rh-negative mother exposed to Rh-positive fetal blood will develop anti-D antibodies, which could cause hemolytic disease of the fetus/newborn (HDFN) in subsequent pregnancies. This applies to all ectopic pregnancies regardless of management approach (medical, surgical, or expectant). Folate is given with methotrexate recovery, not for Rh prophylaxis.

stem: After methotrexate for ectopic, β-hCG should decline by ≥15% between:

choices.feedback: Too early — β-hCG may initially rise after MTX.
Day 4 to 7 comparison is the standard monitoring window.
Monitoring starts earlier than this.

Correct! β-hCG often RISES initially (days 1-4) as trophoblast lyses. Measure on days 4 and 7. If ≥15% decline from day 4 to 7 → successful response → weekly monitoring until zero. If <15% decline → repeat MTX dose or surgery. Treatment failure rate: ~10-15%.

explanation: After methotrexate treatment for ectopic pregnancy, β-hCG should decline by ≥15% between days 4 and 7 post-injection, indicating successful treatment. β-hCG often rises initially between days 1-4 (expected due to continued trophoblast activity before methotrexate effect), so early measurement may be falsely reassuring or alarming. If β-hCG does not decline ≥15% by day 7, a second dose of methotrexate or surgical intervention is needed. Weekly β-hCG monitoring continues until undetectable. Days 1-2 is too early. Days 14-21 would delay recognition of treatment failure.

stem: IUD and ectopic pregnancy — the relationship is:

choices.text: Only hormonal IUDs affect ectopic risk
choices.feedback: Both copper and hormonal IUDs have similar ectopic pregnancy considerations.
IUDs reduce overall pregnancy risk but don't eliminate ectopic risk entirely.
IUDs don't cause ectopics — they prevent intrauterine pregnancy.

choices.text: If pregnancy occurs WITH IUD in place, higher proportion are ectopic
choices.feedback: Correct! IUDs are highly effective at preventing intrauterine pregnancy but less effective at preventing ectopic. If a patient gets pregnant with an IUD, there's a higher proportion of ectopics (not higher absolute risk vs no contraception). Most IUD pregnancies are still intrauterine, but always evaluate for ectopic.

explanation: IUDs are highly effective contraceptives that dramatically reduce overall pregnancy risk (including ectopic). However, IF pregnancy occurs with an IUD in place, a higher proportion of those pregnancies are ectopic compared to pregnancies without an IUD. This is because IUDs are more effective at preventing intrauterine implantation than tubal implantation. IUDs do not cause ectopic pregnancies — they prevent pregnancies overall. This distinction is important for patient counseling. Both hormonal and copper IUDs have this pattern, not only hormonal IUDs.

stem: Most common gynecologic cancer in the US?

choices.feedback: Vulvar cancer is rare.
Cervical cancer incidence has declined significantly due to screening and HPV vaccination.

Correct! Endometrial cancer is #1 in incidence. Ovarian is #1 in mortality. Key risk factor: unopposed estrogen (obesity, PCOS, nulliparity, tamoxifen). Presentation: postmenopausal bleeding → always perform endometrial biopsy.
Ovarian is the deadliest but not the most common.

explanation: Endometrial cancer is the most common gynecologic malignancy in the US, with approximately 65,000 new cases annually. Risk factors include obesity, chronic anovulation (PCOS), nulliparity, unopposed estrogen exposure, tamoxifen use, and Lynch syndrome. The most common presenting symptom is postmenopausal bleeding. Most cases are diagnosed at an early stage (type 1, endometrioid histology) with favorable prognosis (5-year survival >90% for stage I).

stem: #1 cause of gynecologic cancer death?

choices.feedback: Endometrial cancer generally has good prognosis due to early detection.
Uterine sarcomas are aggressive but rare.

Correct! Ovarian cancer is the #1 GYN cancer killer because it presents late (75% stage III-IV at diagnosis). No effective screening test. Vague symptoms (bloating, satiety, pelvic pain) lead to delayed diagnosis. 5-year survival for advanced disease: ~30%.
Cervical cancer deaths declining due to Pap screening and HPV vaccination.

explanation: Ovarian cancer is the leading cause of gynecologic cancer death in the US despite not being the most common gynecologic cancer. This is because most ovarian cancers present at advanced stages (III-IV) due to the absence of reliable screening methods and vague early symptoms. The 5-year survival for stage III/IV is ~30%. CA-125 is used for monitoring but is not an effective screening tool in the general population.

stem: Postmenopausal bleeding warrants what evaluation?

choices.text: Reassurance — spotting is normal after menopause
choices.feedback: ANY postmenopausal bleeding is abnormal and requires evaluation.

Correct! All postmenopausal bleeding needs evaluation to rule out endometrial cancer. Transvaginal US first: endometrial thickness ≤4mm has high negative predictive value. If >4mm or symptomatic → endometrial biopsy (office pipelle). ~10% of postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer.
Imaging alone is insufficient — tissue sampling is needed.
Pap screens for cervical cancer, not endometrial.

explanation: Any postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) requires endometrial biopsy to exclude endometrial cancer, which accounts for ~10% of PMB cases (atrophy is the most common cause at 60-80%). Office endometrial biopsy (Pipelle) is the first-line tissue sampling method. Transvaginal ultrasound measuring endometrial thickness (<4 mm is reassuring) may be used as initial assessment, but biopsy is definitive. Pap smear does not adequately sample the endometrium.

stem: HPV types most associated with cervical cancer?

choices.feedback: These are low-risk HPV types.
HPV 6 and 11 cause genital warts (low-risk), not cancer.
HPV 1 and 2 cause common skin warts.

Correct! HPV 16 and 18 are high-risk types responsible for ~70% of cervical cancers. HPV 16 alone accounts for ~50%. The 9-valent HPV vaccine (Gardasil 9) covers HPV 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58 — preventing ~90% of cervical cancers.

explanation: HPV types 16 and 18 are responsible for approximately 70% of cervical cancers. HPV 16 is the single most oncogenic type. Both are high-risk types that integrate into the host genome, producing E6 and E7 oncoproteins that inactivate p53 and Rb tumor suppressors, respectively. The HPV vaccine (Gardasil 9) covers HPV 16, 18, plus five additional high-risk types (31, 33, 45, 52, 58) and two low-risk types (6, 11 — genital warts).

stem: BRCA1/2 mutation carriers should be offered:

choices.feedback: Correct! BRCA1/2 carriers have 15-45% lifetime ovarian cancer risk. RRSO after completing childbearing reduces ovarian cancer risk by ~80% and breast cancer risk by ~50% (premenopausal oophorectomy). Also: enhanced breast screening (MRI + mammography), consider PARP inhibitors if cancer develops.
BRCA mutations don't increase cervical cancer risk.
Mammograms are important but ovarian cancer risk also needs addressing.
Chemoprevention is not standard for BRCA carriers.

explanation: BRCA1/2 mutation carriers should be offered risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO), ideally by age 35-40 for BRCA1 and by age 40-45 for BRCA2. RRSO reduces ovarian cancer risk by ~80-90% and breast cancer risk by ~50% (when performed premenopausally due to estrogen reduction). Current evidence supports complete salpingectomy over tubal ligation, as many high-grade serous ovarian cancers originate from fallopian tube fimbriae. Genetic counseling and shared decision-making are essential.

stem: CIN 2-3 on colposcopy-directed biopsy. Treatment?

choices.feedback: CIN 1 can be observed. CIN 2-3 requires treatment due to progression risk.
Hysterectomy is for invasive cancer, not CIN.
Topical agents are not standard for CIN treatment.

Correct! CIN 2-3 (high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion) has significant progression risk to invasive cancer. Excision: LEEP (loop electrosurgical excision) preferred, or cold knife cone. Evaluates margins. Follow-up with co-testing at 12 and 24 months. CIN 1: observe (60% regress spontaneously).

explanation: CIN 2-3 (high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia/HSIL) on colposcopy-directed biopsy is treated with excisional procedures: LEEP (loop electrosurgical excision procedure) or cold knife cone biopsy. These remove the transformation zone containing the dysplastic cells and provide tissue for margin assessment. LEEP is most commonly used due to its office-based convenience. Cold knife cone is preferred when invasive cancer cannot be excluded or for adenocarcinoma in situ (AIS). CIN 1 may be observed, but CIN 2-3 requires treatment due to significant progression risk to invasive cancer.

stem: Lynch syndrome increases lifetime risk of which GYN cancer to 40-60%?

choices.feedback: Lynch doesn't significantly increase cervical cancer risk.
Vulvar cancer is not a Lynch-associated cancer.
Lynch increases ovarian risk to ~10-12%, not 40-60%.

Correct! Lynch syndrome (HNPCC): mismatch repair mutations (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) increase endometrial cancer risk to 40-60% (higher than colon cancer risk in women!). Screen: annual endometrial biopsy starting age 30-35. Prophylactic hysterectomy + BSO after childbearing.

stem: Which ultrasound finding is considered most specific for ovarian torsion?

choices.text: Simple anechoic cyst within the ovary
choices.feedback: Simple cysts are common and not specific for torsion.
This can occur with hemorrhagic infarction but is not the most specific finding.
Free fluid can be seen with torsion but is nonspecific (also seen with ruptured cyst, ectopic pregnancy).

choices.text: Whirlpool sign (twisted vascular pedicle on Doppler)
choices.feedback: The whirlpool sign represents the twisted ovarian pedicle seen on color Doppler and is highly specific for torsion. Other findings: enlarged ovary (>5 cm), peripheral follicles displaced to cortex ('string of pearls'), free pelvic fluid, absent or decreased arterial/venous flow. Note: presence of Doppler flow does NOT exclude torsion (intermittent torsion, dual blood supply, or early torsion may preserve some flow).

explanation: Cervical cancer screening: Pap smear starting age 21, co-testing (Pap + HPV) ages 30-65. HPV 16 and 18 cause ~70% of cervical cancers. Endometrial cancer: most common gynecologic malignancy, presents with postmenopausal bleeding. Risk factors: unopposed estrogen, obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen. Diagnosis: endometrial biopsy.

stem: In pediatric patients, what is the most common cause of ovarian torsion?

choices.feedback: Ovarian carcinoma is extremely rare in pediatric patients.
Dermoid cysts are the most common ovarian tumor in young women and can cause torsion in adolescents/adults, but in prepubertal children, normal ovaries are the most common.

In children, ~50% of ovarian torsion occurs with a normal ovary (vs. adults where a cyst/mass is usually present). This is because the long utero-ovarian ligament in prepubertal girls allows greater ovarian mobility. Symptoms may mimic appendicitis. High index of suspicion is needed for any girl with acute lower abdominal/pelvic pain. Oophoropexy may be performed to prevent recurrence.
Endometriomas are uncommon in prepubertal patients.

explanation: Correct: Normal ovary (no pathologic cyst or mass).

stem: After laparoscopic detorsion of a torsed ovary, what follow-up is recommended?

choices.text: No follow-up needed if the ovary was detorsed successfully
choices.feedback: Follow-up imaging is important to confirm ovarian recovery and detect any underlying pathology.

choices.text: Repeat ultrasound in 6 weeks to assess ovarian viability and perfusion
choices.feedback: Follow-up imaging confirms ovarian recovery (normal size, follicular development, restored blood flow). Most conserved ovaries recover function even if they appeared compromised during surgery. If a cyst was the lead point, consider cystectomy at the same or a subsequent procedure. Patients should be counseled about recurrence risk (~10%) and to seek care promptly for future sudden pelvic pain.
Routine bilateral oophoropexy is not standard. It may be considered for recurrent torsion or in pediatric patients.

choices.text: Start oral contraceptives to suppress ovulation permanently
choices.feedback: OCPs may reduce functional cyst formation but are not routinely prescribed after torsion.

stem: A 20-year-old woman presents with bilateral lower abdominal pain, purulent cervical discharge, and cervical motion tenderness. Temperature is 38.5°C. Which organisms are most commonly implicated?

choices.feedback: HSV causes genital ulcers, not ascending pelvic infection.

PID is typically polymicrobial: N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis initiate infection, then vaginal anaerobes (Bacteroides, Prevotella) and facultative organisms (E. coli, streptococci) ascend. Diagnosis is clinical: minimum criteria = lower abdominal pain + cervical motion tenderness or adnexal/uterine tenderness. Additional criteria: fever, mucopurulent discharge, elevated ESR/CRP, positive GC/CT test.
GBS is important in neonatal sepsis and pregnancy but is not a primary PID pathogen.

stem: A patient with PID develops a 7 cm complex adnexal mass with internal debris. She has high fever and peritoneal signs. What is the most likely complication?

choices.feedback: TOA develops in ~15% of PID cases when infection walled off by omentum/bowel creates an abscess. Imaging: complex adnexal mass with internal echoes, septations, fluid-debris levels. Management: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin/sulbactam + doxycycline OR clindamycin + gentamicin). If not improving in 48-72 hours → percutaneous drainage or surgery. Ruptured TOA is a surgical emergency with high mortality.
Ectopic pregnancy causes adnexal mass but with positive pregnancy test and different clinical picture.
Torsion presents with sudden pain and absent Doppler flow, not complex mass with fever and peritoneal signs in the context of PID.
Endometriomas are 'chocolate cysts' associated with endometriosis, not acute PID.

explanation: Correct: Tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA).

stem: What is the most feared long-term complication of PID?

choices.feedback: Cervical stenosis is not a common PID complication.
Prolapse is related to pelvic floor weakness, not PID.

choices.text: Infertility due to tubal scarring and adhesions
choices.feedback: PID-related tubal factor infertility occurs in ~8% after 1 episode, ~20% after 2 episodes, and ~40% after 3+ episodes. Inflammation causes tubal mucosal damage, fimbrial agglutination, and pelvic adhesions. Other long-term complications: ectopic pregnancy (6-10x increased risk), chronic pelvic pain (18%), Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome (perihepatitis). Early treatment is critical to prevent sequelae.
PID does not significantly increase ovarian cancer risk.

explanation: Best answer: Infertility due to tubal scarring and adhesions.

stem: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome is a complication of PID. What does it involve?

choices.feedback: Splenic abscess is not associated with PID or Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome.

choices.text: Perihepatitis with inflammation of the liver capsule
choices.feedback: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome involves inflammation of the hepatic capsule (Glisson's capsule) and adjacent peritoneum, creating fibrous adhesions between the liver and anterior abdominal wall ('violin string' adhesions). Presents with RUQ pain that may mimic cholecystitis. Caused by transperitoneal or lymphatic spread of N. gonorrhoeae or C. trachomatis. CT may show hepatic capsule enhancement.
Asherman syndrome (intrauterine adhesions) results from endometrial trauma, not perihepatitis.

choices.text: Tubo-ovarian abscess rupture into the peritoneum
choices.feedback: TOA rupture is a separate complication of PID, not Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome.

explanation: Best answer: Perihepatitis with inflammation of the liver capsule and adhesions ('violin string' adhesions).

stem: Stage 1 of labor is divided into latent and active phases. The active phase begins at:

choices.text: 6 cm cervical dilation (with regular contractions and cervical change)
choices.feedback: Correct! Current definition (ACOG): active labor begins at 6 cm. The latent phase (0–6 cm) is variable and may be prolonged, especially in nulliparas. Active phase arrest: no cervical change for ≥6 hours with adequate contractions, or ≥4 hours with oxytocin and ruptured membranes.

explanation:
Stage 1 of labor: cervical dilation from 0 to 10 cm. Latent phase (0–6 cm): irregular/mild contractions, slow cervical change, variable duration (can last hours to days in nulliparas). Active phase (6–10 cm): strong regular contractions, more rapid cervical dilation (~1-2 cm/hour). ACOG updated the active phase threshold from 4 cm to 6 cm to reduce unnecessary cesarean sections for "failure to progress" in latent labor. Active phase arrest is diagnosed when: ≥6 hours of adequate contractions without cervical change, or ≥4 hours of augmentation with oxytocin and amniotomy without change. Intervention before these thresholds is considered premature.

choices.text: Manual extraction of the placenta under anesthesia
choices.feedback: Correct! If the placenta is not delivered within 30 minutes, manual removal is indicated to prevent hemorrhage. Technique: hand enters uterus, fingers find cleavage plane between placenta and uterine wall, and the placenta is manually separated and removed.

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 34 weeks gestation presents with new-onset headache, visual changes (scotomata), and right upper quadrant pain. She has been healthy throughout pregnancy until now.Vital SignsBP 168/110 (was 118/72 at last visit)HR 92LabsProtein_Cr_ratio 0.8 (significant proteinuria)AST 186 U/LALT 198 U/LPlatelets 92,000Cr 1.2 mg/dLLDH 420 U/L What is the diagnosis and most critical management?

choices.text: Gestational hypertension — monitor with antihypertensives and deliver at 37 weeks
Preeclampsia with severe features (HELLP overlap) — IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis + delivery is the DEFINITIVE treatment at ≥34 weeks
Acute fatty liver of pregnancy — liver failure requiring transplant evaluation
Chronic hypertension with superimposed preeclampsia — continue outpatient monitoring
Labetalol alone is sufficient to manage this presentation

explanation:
Correct Answer: Preeclampsia with severe features: new-onset HTN (≥140/90) after 20 weeks gestation + proteinuria + ≥1 severe feature. Severe features: BP ≥160/110, headache/visual changes (cerebral edema), RUQ pain (hepatic capsule distension), elevated transaminases (≥2x ULN), platelets 1.1, pulmonary edema. HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis + Elevated Liver enzymes + Low Platelets) = severe variant. Management: (1) IV magnesium sulfate (seizure prophylaxis — eclampsia prevention), (2) Antihypertensives (labetalol, hydralazine, nifedipine for acute BP control), (3) DELIVERY = only definitive cure. At ≥34 weeks with severe features: deliver. At <34 weeks: consider 48h of corticosteroids for fetal lung maturation if stable.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- New HTN (168/110) after 20 weeks + proteinuria = preeclampsia
- Headache + scotomata = cerebral involvement (severe feature)
- RUQ pain + AST/ALT ~190 = hepatic involvement (severe feature)
- Platelets 92K (<100K) = HELLP overlap
- 34 weeks gestation with severe features = DELIVER (no benefit to expectant management)
- MgSO4: prevents eclampsia (seizures) — the #1 cause of maternal mortality from preeclampsia

Evidence: Preeclampsia - StatPearls - Rana S, et al. StatPearls. NBK570611. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Gestational hypertension: Gestational HTN is new HTN after 20 weeks WITHOUT proteinuria or end-organ damage. This patient has proteinuria (P/Cr 0.8), elevated transaminases, thrombocytopenia, and neurologic symptoms = preeclampsia with severe features, not simple gestational HTN.
- Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP): AFLP can overlap with HELLP (both cause elevated LFTs + thrombocytopenia in late pregnancy), but AFLP is characterized by hypoglycemia, hypofibrinogenemia, and elevated bilirubin leading to hepatic failure. This patient's presentation (HTN, proteinuria, headache) is more consistent with preeclampsia/HELLP.
- Chronic HTN with superimposed preeclampsia: Chronic HTN exists before 20 weeks or persists >12 weeks postpartum. This patient had normal BP (118/72) throughout pregnancy until now. New-onset HTN at 34 weeks = preeclampsia, not chronic HTN. More importantly, the management of severe features is the same regardless.
- Labetalol alone is sufficient: Labetalol controls BP but does NOT prevent seizures and does NOT address the underlying placental pathology. MgSO4 is essential for seizure prophylaxis, and DELIVERY is the only definitive treatment. Antihypertensives are a component of management, not the sole treatment.

Reference: Preeclampsia - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK570611/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 29-year-old woman with a history of prior PID and IUD use presents with 7 weeks of amenorrhea, unilateral left lower quadrant pain, and light vaginal bleeding. She reports dizziness when standing.Vital SignsBP 92/58HR 118Labsβ-hCG 2,800 mIU/mLTransvaginal US No intrauterine pregnancy; 3.2 cm left adnexal mass with “ring of fire” on Doppler; moderate free fluid in cul-de-sacHgb 9.2 g/dL (was 12.8 at last check) What is the diagnosis and most critical next step?

choices.text: Threatened abortion — conservative management with serial β-hCG monitoring
Ruptured ectopic pregnancy — emergent surgical intervention (salpingectomy or salpingostomy) for hemodynamic instability + free
Administer methotrexate for medical management of ectopic pregnancy
Repeat β-hCG in 48 hours to assess for pregnancy of unknown location
Corpus luteum cyst — normal early pregnancy finding

explanation:
Correct Answer: Ectopic pregnancy with hemodynamic instability (BP 92/58, HR 118) + free fluid (hemoperitoneum) = RUPTURED ectopic requiring emergent surgery. The discriminating zone: β-hCG >1,500-2,000 with NO intrauterine pregnancy on TVUS = ectopic until proven otherwise. Methotrexate criteria: hemodynamically STABLE, unruptured, β-hCG <5,000, no fetal cardiac activity, mass <3.5cm, no contraindications. This patient FAILS methotrexate criteria (hypotensive, free fluid = ruptured). Risk factors: prior PID (#1), prior ectopic, IUD, tubal surgery, endometriosis, ART.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- β-hCG 2,800 (above discriminatory zone ~1,500-2,000) + no IUP on TVUS = ectopic
- Left adnexal mass with ring of fire (increased vascularity) = ectopic pregnancy
- Free fluid in cul-de-sac = hemoperitoneum from rupture
- Hemodynamic instability (BP 92/58, HR 118) = hemorrhagic shock
- Hgb drop (12.8 → 9.2) = significant blood loss
- FAILS methotrexate criteria → emergent surgery (salpingectomy preferred if no desire for future fertility; salpingostomy if fertility desired)

Evidence: Ectopic Pregnancy - StatPearls - Hendriks E, et al. StatPearls. NBK539860. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Threatened abortion: Threatened abortion shows an intrauterine pregnancy with bleeding. This patient has NO IUP on TVUS with β-hCG above the discriminatory zone = ectopic, not threatened abortion. The adnexal mass and free fluid confirm the ectopic location.
- Methotrexate: Methotrexate is appropriate for STABLE, unruptured ectopic pregnancy. This patient is hemodynamically UNSTABLE (BP 92/58, HR 118) with free fluid (hemoperitoneum from rupture) and significant anemia. Methotrexate in a ruptured ectopic would allow continued hemorrhage.
- Repeat β-hCG in 48 hours: Serial β-hCG monitoring is appropriate for pregnancy of unknown location when β-hCG is BELOW the discriminatory zone and the patient is STABLE. This patient has β-hCG above discriminatory zone with no IUP = ectopic, AND she is hemodynamically unstable. Waiting 48 hours risks death from hemorrhage.
- Corpus luteum cyst: Corpus luteum cysts occur in early pregnancy and can cause unilateral pain, but they should be accompanied by an intrauterine pregnancy. The combination of NO IUP + adnexal mass + free fluid + hemodynamic instability = ruptured ectopic, not a benign corpus luteum cyst.

Reference: Ectopic Pregnancy - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539860/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 26 weeks gestation undergoes routine screening. Her 50-g glucose challenge test (GCT) result is 156 mg/dL. She then undergoes a 100-g 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT).OGTT ResultsFasting 98 mg/dL (≥95)“1 hour” 192 mg/dL (≥180)“2 hour” 168 mg/dL (≥155)“3 hour” 138 mg/dL ( How is gestational diabetes diagnosed, and what is the initial management?

choices.text: Normal results — only one value is above the cutoff
Pre-existing type 2 diabetes unmasked by pregnancy
Start metformin immediately — first-line for GDM
Repeat the glucose challenge test in the third trimester for confirmation

explanation:
Correct Answer: GDM diagnosis: Two-step screening: (1) 50g GCT at 24-28 weeks (cutoff ≥130-140 mg/dL); (2) if positive, 100g 3-hour OGTT (Carpenter-Coustan criteria: fasting ≥95, 1h ≥180, 2h ≥155, 3h ≥140). ≥2 abnormal values = GDM. Initial management: medical nutrition therapy (MNT) + self-monitored blood glucose (fasting insulin is first-line pharmacotherapy (does not cross placenta). Metformin and glyburide are alternatives but DO cross the placenta.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 50g GCT 156 (positive screening >140) → 3-hour OGTT
- OGTT: 3 of 4 values abnormal (fasting 98, 1h 192, 2h 168) → ≥2 needed = GDM confirmed
- 26 weeks = appropriate screening window (24-28 weeks)
- Initial therapy: diet modification (MNT) + glucose monitoring
- If diet fails (1-2 weeks): insulin (first-line pharmacotherapy; does not cross placenta)

Evidence: GDM - StatPearls - Quintanilla Rodriguez BS, et al. StatPearls. NBK545196. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Normal results: The diagnosis of GDM requires ≥2 abnormal values on the 3-hour OGTT. This patient has THREE abnormal values (fasting 98 ≥95, 1h 192 ≥180, 2h 168 ≥155). Only the 3-hour value (138) is normal. The diagnosis is clearly confirmed.
- Pre-existing type 2 diabetes: Pre-existing T2DM is suggested by markedly elevated glucose in the FIRST trimester (<20 weeks): fasting ≥126 or A1c ≥6.5% or random glucose ≥200. This patient's abnormal results at 26 weeks on standard screening suggest GDM, which by definition is glucose intolerance first recognized during pregnancy.
- Start metformin immediately: While metformin is an alternative for GDM treatment, it is NOT first-line. Initial management is ALWAYS medical nutrition therapy (diet) + glucose monitoring. If diet fails after 1-2 weeks, INSULIN is the preferred first-line pharmacotherapy (it does not cross the placenta). Metformin crosses the placenta and long-term offspring effects are being studied.
- Repeat the glucose challenge test: The 3-hour OGTT is the CONFIRMATORY test. With ≥2 abnormal values, GDM is diagnosed — no repeat testing needed. Repeating the GCT would unnecessarily delay treatment and expose the fetus to continued uncontrolled hyperglycemia.

Reference: GDM - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK545196/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 30-year-old G3P2 at 35 weeks gestation with chronic hypertension and cocaine use presents with sudden-onset severe constant abdominal pain and dark vaginal bleeding. She reports the pain started 1 hour ago and is unremitting. The uterus is rigid ("board-like") and tender to palpation. Fetal heart tracing shows recurrent late decelerations with decreased variability.Vital SignsBP 160/105HR 118Temp 37.0°CLabsHgb 8.9 g/dL (was 11.8 yesterday)Platelets 108,000Fibrinogen 145 mg/dL (normal >200 in pregnancy)INR 1.4Fetal HR Recurrent late decelerations What is the diagnosis, and what complication is developing?

choices.text: Placenta previa — painless bright red bleeding requiring cesarean
Placental abruption with disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) —
Uterine rupture — loss of uterine contour with fetal parts palpable
Normal bloody show preceding labor at 35 weeks
Vasa previa — fetal vessel rupture with fetal hemorrhage

explanation:
Correct Answer: Placental abruption vs placenta previa: the critical discriminator is PAIN. Abruption = PAINFUL, dark bleeding, rigid uterus. Previa = PAINLESS, bright red bleeding, soft uterus. Abruption risk factors: HTN (#1), cocaine, trauma, prior abruption, advanced maternal age. This case shows developing DIC: fibrinogen 150-200).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Sudden painful dark vaginal bleeding at 35 weeks = abruption until proven otherwise
- Board-like rigid uterus = myometrial infiltration by blood (Couvelaire uterus)
- Cocaine use + chronic HTN = two major abruption risk factors
- Fibrinogen 145 (critically low for pregnancy where normal is 400-600) + platelets 108K + INR 1.4 = DIC
- Recurrent late decelerations = fetal distress from uteroplacental insufficiency
- Hgb dropping (11.8 → 8.9) = significant hemorrhage

Evidence: Placental Abruption - StatPearls - Schmidt P, et al. StatPearls. NBK482335. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Placenta previa: Previa causes PAINLESS, bright red bleeding with a soft non-tender uterus. This patient has painful dark bleeding with a board-like rigid uterus = abruption, not previa. Previa is diagnosed on ultrasound (low-lying placenta over cervical os). The presence of DIC further confirms abruption.
- Uterine rupture: Uterine rupture causes sudden pain with loss of fetal station, palpable fetal parts through the abdominal wall, and loss of uterine contour. It typically occurs during labor in patients with a prior uterine scar. This patient has an intact rigid uterus with no prior cesarean, consistent with abruption.
- Bloody show: Bloody show is a small amount of blood-tinged mucus that occurs as the cervix dilates before labor. It is NOT associated with severe constant pain, rigid uterus, large-volume dark bleeding, or DIC. The severity of this presentation is far beyond normal labor-associated bleeding.
- Vasa previa: Vasa previa involves fetal blood vessels running through the membranes over the cervical os. Rupture causes FETAL hemorrhage (sinusoidal fetal heart pattern, fetal anemia) with relatively little maternal blood loss. This patient has MATERNAL hemorrhage (dropping Hgb, DIC) and maternal hemodynamic compromise, consistent with abruption.

Reference: Placental Abruption - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482335/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 23-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset severe right lower quadrant pain that started 4 hours ago while exercising. The pain is intermittent and colicky, associated with nausea and vomiting. She reports that the pain "comes and goes" in waves. Her last menstrual period was 2 weeks ago (mid-cycle). Pregnancy test is negative.Vital SignsTemp 37.4°CHR 102ExamAbdomen RLQ tenderness with guardingPelvic Right adnexal tenderness with a palpable fullnessDoppler US 8 cm right ovarian cyst with decreased/absent arterial flow to the right ovary; free fluid in cul-de-sacLeft ovary Normal size and flow What is the diagnosis, and what distinguishes this from appendicitis?

choices.text: Ovarian torsion — the ovary twists on its vascular pedicle, causing ischemia;
Appendicitis — periumbilical pain migrating to RLQ with fever
Ectopic pregnancy — must rule out with pregnancy test
Pelvic inflammatory disease — bilateral adnexal tenderness with cervical motion tenderness

explanation:
Correct Answer: Ovarian torsion: the ovary (with or without the fallopian tube) twists on its vascular pedicle (infundibulopelvic and utero-ovarian ligaments) → venous congestion then arterial compromise → ischemia → necrosis. Key features distinguishing from appendicitis: (1) Sudden-onset COLICKY pain that waxes and wanes (torsion-detorsion cycles, vs appendicitis which is constant and progressive), (2) Often mid-cycle or with a known ovarian mass (lead point), (3) Doppler US: decreased/absent ovarian blood flow, (4) Early: NO fever, NO leukocytosis (fever appears late with necrosis). Risk factor: ovarian cyst or mass >5 cm. Treatment: emergent laparoscopic detorsion (even if ovary appears dusky/black, detorsion is attempted first — ovaries often recover). Oophoropexy may prevent recurrence.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Sudden severe RLQ pain in reproductive-age woman = ovarian torsion or ectopic until proven otherwise
- Colicky, waxing-waning pain = torsion-detorsion cycles (intermittent twisting)
- Mid-cycle (2 weeks after LMP) = corpus luteum cyst as potential lead point
- 8 cm ovarian cyst = lead point (cysts >5 cm increase torsion risk)
- Decreased/absent Doppler flow = vascular compromise from twisting
- Negative pregnancy test = rules out ectopic pregnancy

Evidence: Ovarian Torsion - StatPearls - Sasaki KJ, et al. StatPearls. NBK560714. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Ruptured ovarian cyst: Ruptured ovarian cyst causes sudden pelvic pain and free fluid, but pain typically PEAKS at onset then gradually IMPROVES (one-time event). Torsion pain PERSISTS and is colicky (waxes/wanes). Most critically, ruptured cyst shows NORMAL ovarian blood flow on Doppler, while torsion shows decreased/absent flow.
- Appendicitis: Appendicitis causes periumbilical pain that MIGRATES to RLQ over 12-24 hours (constant, progressive, not colicky), fever, anorexia, and leukocytosis. Appendicitis does NOT cause adnexal fullness or decreased ovarian Doppler flow. CT (not pelvic US) is the primary imaging for appendicitis.
- Ectopic pregnancy: Ectopic pregnancy is correctly ruled out by the NEGATIVE pregnancy test. Ectopic causes unilateral pain + amenorrhea + positive β-hCG + adnexal mass. Always check pregnancy test in reproductive-age women with pelvic pain, but it is definitively negative here.
- PID: PID causes BILATERAL lower abdominal/pelvic pain, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent discharge, and fever. It develops over days (not sudden onset). PID is NOT typically colicky and does NOT cause decreased ovarian Doppler flow.

Reference: Ovarian Torsion - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560714/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 at 39 weeks presents with regular contractions every 4 minutes, lasting 60 seconds, for the past 3 hours. She reports her "water broke" 1 hour ago with clear fluid. Cervical exam: 5 cm dilated, 80% effaced, -1 station, vertex presentation. Fetal heart rate tracing shows a baseline of 140 with moderate variability and accelerations.ExamCervix 5 cm / 80% / -1 stationContractions q4 min x 60 secFetal HR 140 bpm, moderate variability, accelerations presentAmniotic fluid Clear, positive ferning + nitrazine What stage and phase of labor is this patient in, and what cervical change defines the transition between phases?

choices.text: Second stage of labor — begins after full dilation
First stage, ACTIVE phase — cervix is 6 cm or greater (current ACOG definition);
First stage, latent phase — lasts until 4 cm dilation per older criteria
Third stage — delivery of the placenta
Prolonged latent phase requiring immediate cesarean delivery

explanation:
Correct Answer: Stages of labor: Stage 1 (cervical dilation): latent phase (0 to 6 cm, slow/irregular) → active phase (6 to 10 cm, regular, ≥0.5 cm/h). Stage 2 (pushing): full dilation to delivery. Stage 3: delivery to placenta expulsion (ACOG/Safe Prevention of Primary Cesarean (2014) redefined active labor as starting at 6 cm (not the older Friedman curve 4 cm). Arrest of active labor: cervix ≥6 cm + ruptured membranes + no change for ≥4 hours with adequate contractions (200+ Montevideo units) OR ≥6 hours without adequate contractions. This patient at 5 cm is in late latent phase with ruptured membranes and reassuring fetal status — expectant management is appropriate.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 5 cm dilated = LATENT phase by current ACOG definition (active starts at 6 cm)
- Regular contractions q4 min x 60 sec = adequate labor pattern
- Ruptured membranes (positive ferning + nitrazine) confirmed = SROM
- Category I tracing (baseline 140, moderate variability, accelerations) = reassuring
- Expectant management: allow labor to progress to 6 cm before diagnosing arrest
- The key update: old Friedman curve said active at 4 cm; ACOG now says 6 cm

Evidence: Stages of Labor - StatPearls - Hutchison J, et al. StatPearls. NBK544290. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Second stage: Second stage begins at COMPLETE cervical dilation (10 cm) and ends with delivery of the infant. This patient is only 5 cm dilated, so she is still in the first stage.
- First stage latent phase until 4 cm: This reflects the OLDER Friedman curve definition. The current ACOG consensus (2014, Safe Prevention of Primary Cesarean) redefined active labor as beginning at 6 cm, not 4 cm. This change was made to reduce unnecessary cesarean deliveries for arrest disorders. At 5 cm, this patient is in latent phase by current standards.
- Third stage: Third stage of labor is from delivery of the infant to delivery of the placenta. Normal duration: 30 minutes. This patient has not yet delivered.
- Prolonged latent phase requiring cesarean: There is NO maximum duration for the latent phase by current ACOG guidelines. Prolonged latent phase alone is NEVER an indication for cesarean delivery. Cesarean is indicated for arrest in the ACTIVE phase (≥6 cm) after appropriate time with adequate contractions.

Reference: Stages of Labor - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK544290/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 with gestational diabetes (estimated fetal weight 4,400g) is in the second stage of labor. After delivery of the fetal head, the chin retracts tightly against the perineum (TURTLE SIGN) and the anterior shoulder does not deliver with gentle downward traction. The fetal head has been delivered for 45 seconds. What is the immediate sequence of maneuvers?

choices.text: Apply fundal pressure to push the baby out
Pull harder on the fetal head with lateral traction
Episiotomy alone will resolve the dystocia

explanation:
Correct Answer: Shoulder dystocia: the anterior fetal shoulder is impacted behind the maternal pubic symphysis after delivery of the head. TURTLE SIGN: fetal head delivers then retracts against perineum. This is an EMERGENCY (umbilical cord compression, risk of brachial plexus injury, fetal hypoxia). Management sequence: HELPERR mnemonic: (H) call for Help, (E) Evaluate for Episiotomy, (L) Legs — McRoberts (hyperflexion of maternal hips, most effective single maneuver), (P) suprapubic Pressure (direct pressure above pubic symphysis to dislodge anterior shoulder — NEVER fundal pressure), (E) Enter — rotational maneuvers (Rubin II, Wood's screw), (R) Remove posterior arm, (R) Roll to all-fours (Gaskin). NEVER: fundal pressure (worsens impaction), excessive lateral traction on head (brachial plexus injury).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Turtle sign (head retracts against perineum) = shoulder dystocia
- GDM + EFW 4,400g = macrosomia risk factor
- 45 seconds since head delivery = time-sensitive (goal: delivery within 5 minutes)
- McRoberts first (simplest, most effective single maneuver, 40-90% success)
- Suprapubic pressure = adjunct to McRoberts (push anterior shoulder inferiorly)
- NEVER fundal pressure (pushes shoulder further into symphysis)

Evidence: Shoulder Dystocia - StatPearls - Gherman RB, et al. StatPearls. NBK470427. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Fundal pressure: Fundal pressure is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in shoulder dystocia. It pushes the anterior shoulder MORE firmly into the pubic symphysis, worsening impaction and increasing risk of brachial plexus injury. SUPRAPUBIC pressure (directed above the symphysis, pushing the anterior shoulder inferiorly) is the correct external maneuver.
- Immediate cesarean: Cesarean delivery is NOT possible after the fetal head has already delivered vaginally. The head is outside the birth canal. The Zavanelli maneuver (replacing the head into the vagina followed by cesarean) is a LAST RESORT used only when all other maneuvers have failed.
- Pull harder on the fetal head: Excessive lateral traction on the fetal head is the PRIMARY cause of brachial plexus injury (Erb-Duchenne palsy: C5-C6). The maneuvers (McRoberts, suprapubic pressure, rotational maneuvers) work by CHANGING the relationship of the fetal shoulders to the pelvis, not by pulling harder.
- Episiotomy alone: Episiotomy may be considered as part of the HELPERR algorithm (creates more room for internal maneuvers), but it does NOT resolve shoulder dystocia by itself. Shoulder dystocia is a BONY impaction (shoulder behind symphysis), not a soft tissue problem. Episiotomy addresses soft tissue but not the bone-on-bone obstruction.

Reference: Shoulder Dystocia - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470427/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 34-year-old G3P3 delivered vaginally 20 minutes ago after a prolonged labor with oxytocin augmentation. The placenta was delivered intact. She is now bleeding heavily from the vagina. The uterine fundus is palpated above the umbilicus and feels soft and "boggy."Vital SignsBP 88/54HR 128Estimated blood loss0 11 ,2 23 04 05  6 m7 L8  9 i10 n11  12 213 014  15 m16 i17 n18 u19 t20 e21 s What is the most common cause and initial management?

choices.text: Uterine atony (most common cause, ~80% of PPH) — boggy uterus that fails to contract;
Genital tract laceration — the uterus should be firm if this is the cause
DIC developing postpartum — check coagulation studies only
Observation — some bleeding is normal after delivery

explanation:
Correct Answer: Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH): >500 mL after vaginal delivery or >1000 mL after cesarean, or any amount causing hemodynamic instability. 4 T's: Tone (atony, 80% of PPH), Trauma (lacerations, 10%), Tissue (retained placenta, 5%), Thrombin (coagulopathy, 5%). Uterine atony: boggy fundus that fails to contract. Risk factors: prolonged labor, oxytocin use, overdistension (macrosomia, polyhydramnios, multiples), grand multiparity. Management sequence: bimanual uterine massage → uterotonics: (1) oxytocin 20-40 units in 1L NS IV, (2) methylergonovine 0.2 mg IM (contraindicated in HTN), (3) carboprost 250 mcg IM (contraindicated in asthma), (4) misoprostol 600-1000 mcg rectal. Tranexamic acid 1g IV within 3 hours (WOMAN trial: reduces death from PPH by 31%). If refractory: Bakri balloon tamponade, uterine compression sutures (B-Lynch), arterial embolization, or hysterectomy as last resort.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Boggy uterus above umbilicus = uterine atony (fundus should be firm and at/below umbilicus)
- Prolonged labor + oxytocin use = risk factors for atony (fatigued myometrium)
- EBL 1,200 mL with hypotension/tachycardia = hemorrhagic shock
- Placenta delivered intact = less likely retained tissue
- Bimanual massage = immediate first step while uterotonics are prepared
- Tranexamic acid within 3 hours is now standard of care (WOMAN trial)

Evidence: PPH - StatPearls - Evensen A, et al. StatPearls. NBK499988. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Retained placental fragments: The placenta was delivered INTACT, making retained tissue less likely. Retained tissue (the second T) causes PPH when placental fragments prevent uterine contraction at the implantation site. If the placenta was incomplete on inspection, manual exploration and curettage would be indicated.
- Genital tract laceration: If a laceration were the cause, the uterus would be FIRM and well-contracted (not boggy). The boggy uterus palpated above the umbilicus definitively identifies atony as the primary cause. Lacerations should still be systematically inspected and repaired, but the boggy uterus must be addressed first.
- DIC developing postpartum: While DIC can cause or worsen PPH, it is the LEAST common of the 4 T's (~5%). The immediate clinical finding of a boggy uterus points to atony. Coagulation studies should be checked, but treatment for atony (massage + uterotonics) must begin immediately while labs are pending.
- Observation: 1,200 mL blood loss with BP 88/54 and HR 128 = Class III hemorrhagic shock. This is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate intervention, not observation. Normal blood loss after vaginal delivery is 300-500 mL; this patient has lost over twice the upper limit.

Reference: PPH - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499988/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 34 weeks gestation presents with worsening right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and malaise over 2 days. She was diagnosed with gestational hypertension at 30 weeks. BP is now 162/108. She has facial edema and 3+ proteinuria. Labs are remarkable.Vital SignsBP 162/108HR 96Temp 37.0°CLabsPlatelets 68,000 (was 210,000 last week)AST 312 U/LALT 278 U/LLDH 648 U/LHaptoglobin Hgb 9.8 g/dL (was 12.1)Peripheral smear Schistocytes presentUrine protein 3+Cr 1.2 mg/dL What is the diagnosis, and what distinguishes it from isolated preeclampsia with severe features?

choices.text: Gestational hypertension that has not progressed
HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) — a severe variant of preeclampsia;
Acute fatty liver of pregnancy — hypoglycemia and coagulopathy
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) — pentad with neurologic findings
Isolated cholestasis of pregnancy — pruritus with bile acid elevation

explanation:
Correct Answer: HELLP syndrome vs isolated severe preeclampsia: both share HTN + proteinuria, but HELLP adds the specific triad of Hemolysis (schistocytes, LDH >600, haptoglobin undetectable), Elevated Liver enzymes (AST/ALT >2x upper limit), and Low Platelets (RUQ pain = hepatic capsule distension from subcapsular hemorrhage/edema (most dangerous complication: hepatic rupture, rare but catastrophic). Differential: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy (AFLP): also has liver dysfunction BUT features HYPOGLYCEMIA, prolonged PT/aPTT, and low fibrinogen (DIC), which HELLP typically lacks. TTP: ADAMTS13 Management: delivery is definitive; ≥34 weeks = deliver; <34 weeks = deliver if maternal instability or fetal distress; corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity if time permits; magnesium sulfate; BP control (labetalol, hydralazine, nifedipine).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- HTN 162/108 + proteinuria 3+ = preeclampsia with severe features
- Platelets 68,000 = thrombocytopenia (<100K = HELLP criterion)
- AST 312, ALT 278 = markedly elevated liver enzymes (HELLP criterion)
- LDH 648, haptoglobin undetectable, schistocytes = microangiopathic hemolysis (HELLP criterion)
- All 3 HELLP criteria met: hemolysis + elevated liver enzymes + low platelets
- RUQ pain = hepatic capsule distension (hallmark symptom of HELLP)
- 34 weeks = viable; delivery is definitive treatment

Evidence: HELLP Syndrome - StatPearls - Dusse LMS, et al. StatPearls. NBK560615. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Gestational hypertension not progressed: This has clearly progressed beyond gestational hypertension. The presence of proteinuria (3+) upgrades the diagnosis to preeclampsia. The addition of hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and thrombocytopenia further identifies HELLP syndrome. Gestational hypertension is BP ≥140/90 WITHOUT proteinuria or end-organ damage.
- Acute fatty liver of pregnancy: AFLP shares some features with HELLP (liver dysfunction, thrombocytopenia) but is distinguished by: HYPOGLYCEMIA (key differentiator — HELLP does NOT cause hypoglycemia), prolonged PT/aPTT (coagulopathy from synthetic liver failure, not consumption), low fibrinogen, elevated ammonia, and polydipsia/polyuria. AFLP is a primary liver disease; HELLP is endothelial.
- TTP: TTP causes microangiopathic hemolytic anemia and thrombocytopenia (overlap with HELLP) but is distinguished by: ADAMTS13 activity <10%, prominent NEUROLOGIC symptoms (confusion, seizures, focal deficits), fever, and renal impairment. TTP does not typically cause marked transaminase elevation. ADAMTS13 level differentiates definitively.
- Cholestasis of pregnancy: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP) presents with intense PRURITUS (especially palms/soles) and elevated bile acids. Transaminases may be mildly elevated but NOT to the degree seen here (312/278). ICP does NOT cause hemolysis, thrombocytopenia, or RUQ pain from hepatic capsule distension.

Reference: HELLP Syndrome - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560615/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 36-year-old G2P1 presents at 11 weeks gestation for her first prenatal visit. She asks about screening options for chromosomal abnormalities. Her first child is healthy. She has no family history of genetic disorders.Options presentedOption A First trimester combined screen: nuchal translucency US + serum PAPP-A + free β-hCG (11-13 weeks)Option B Cell-free fetal DNA (cfDNA/NIPT) from maternal blood (can be done from 10 weeks)Option C Quad screen (15-22 weeks)Option D Chorionic villus sampling (CVS, 10-13 weeks)Option E Amniocentesis (15-20 weeks) Which option is SCREENING vs DIAGNOSTIC, and which has the highest sensitivity for trisomy 21?

choices.text: All options are equivalent screening tests
cfDNA (NIPT) has the HIGHEST SENSITIVITY for trisomy 21 (≥99%) among SCREENING tests
First trimester combined screen is the most accurate test available
Amniocentesis should be done first as a screening test for all women
Prenatal screening is only recommended for women over 35

explanation:
Correct Answer: Screening vs Diagnostic: this is the CRITICAL distinction. Screening tests (non-invasive, estimate risk): cfDNA/NIPT (highest sensitivity: >99% for T21, ~97% for T18, ~92% for T13), first trimester combined screen (NT + PAPP-A + β-hCG; detection rate ~82-87% for T21), quad screen (AFP + hCG + estriol + inhibin A; detection rate ~81% for T21). Diagnostic tests (invasive, definitive karyotype): CVS (10-13 weeks, placental villi sampling; 0.5-1% miscarriage risk) and amniocentesis (15-20 weeks, amniotic fluid; 0.1-0.3% miscarriage risk). A positive SCREEN must ALWAYS be confirmed with a DIAGNOSTIC test before any irreversible decisions. ACOG (2020): offer screening AND diagnostic testing to ALL patients regardless of age. cfDNA has dramatically reduced the false-positive rate (0.04% for T21 vs 5% for quad screen).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 36 years old = advanced maternal age (formerly the threshold for offering invasive testing, now all patients offered both)
- 11 weeks = eligible for cfDNA (from 10 weeks) and first trimester combined screen (11-13 weeks)
- cfDNA: highest sensitivity (>99% for T21) with lowest false-positive rate (0.04%)
- But cfDNA is STILL A SCREEN — must confirm positive results with CVS or amniocentesis
- CVS available at 10-13 weeks; amniocentesis at 15-20 weeks
- Patient should receive counseling about ALL options (screening AND diagnostic)

Evidence: Prenatal Screening - ACOG via StatPearls - Rose NC, et al. StatPearls. NBK570611. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- All options equivalent: The tests have vastly different sensitivities, specificities, and clinical roles. cfDNA has >99% sensitivity for T21 (screening); CVS and amniocentesis provide definitive diagnosis (100% — they examine actual fetal chromosomes). First trimester combined screen has ~82-87% detection rate. These are NOT equivalent.
- First trimester combined screen most accurate: First trimester combined screen detects ~82-87% of T21 with a 5% false-positive rate. cfDNA detects >99% with a 0.04% false-positive rate. cfDNA is clearly superior for screening accuracy. However, neither is diagnostic — only CVS and amniocentesis provide definitive karyotype.
- Amniocentesis first as screening: Amniocentesis is a DIAGNOSTIC test (not screening) and carries a 0.1-0.3% miscarriage risk. Using an invasive procedure as a first-line screen would expose many women to unnecessary risk. The appropriate sequence is: non-invasive screening first → invasive diagnostic testing only if screening is positive or if the patient elects diagnostic testing directly.
- Only for women over 35: ACOG 2020 guidelines recommend offering BOTH screening and diagnostic testing to ALL pregnant patients regardless of age. The old practice of using age 35 as a cutoff for offering invasive testing has been replaced by universal access to both screening and diagnostic options. While trisomy risk increases with age, younger women have more pregnancies and account for the majority of affected births.

Reference: Prenatal Screening - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK570611/ [Accessed 2025]
