stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset LEFT lower abdominal pain and vaginal spotting. Her last menstrual period was 7 weeks ago. She has left adnexal tenderness and cervical motion tenderness on exam.Vital SignsBP 102/68HR 108Labsβ-hCG 4,800 mIU/mL (positive)Hgb 10.2 (was 12.8 last month)UltrasoundFinding Empty uterus, left adnexal mass with free fluid in pelvis What is the diagnosis and management based on hemodynamic status?

choices.text: Ruptured ECTOPIC PREGNANCY — β-hCG positive + empty uterus + adnexal mass + free fluid +
Pelvic inflammatory disease causing the symptoms
Wait for β-hCG to double before diagnosis
Methotrexate is first-line regardless of hemodynamic status

explanation:
Correct Answer: Ectopic pregnancy PIVOT from appendicitis: Both present with acute lower abdominal pain, but:
• β-hCG POSITIVE → always consider ectopic in reproductive-age women
• LLQ vs RLQ (though ectopic can be right-sided too)
• Vaginal bleeding (not typical of appendicitis)
• Adnexal mass on US, empty uterus
• Cervical motion tenderness (suggests peritoneal blood)

Management by stability:
• UNSTABLE: surgery immediately (don't delay for labs)
• STABLE + unruptured: methotrexate if criteria met
• STABLE + uncertain: serial β-hCG (should double q48h in viable IUP)

Discriminating zone: β-hCG >1,500-2,000 should show gestational sac on transvaginal US if IUP.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Positive β-hCG + abdominal pain + vaginal bleeding = ectopic until proven otherwise
- 7 weeks since LMP = appropriate timing
- Empty uterus at β-hCG 4,800 = abnormal (should see sac by 1,500-2,000)
- Adnexal mass + free fluid = ruptured ectopic
- HR 108 + borderline BP + Hgb drop = hemodynamic instability
- Unstable = immediate surgery

Evidence: Ectopic Pregnancy - StatPearls - Hendriks E, et al. StatPearls. NBK539860. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Threatened miscarriage: Threatened miscarriage occurs with INTRAUTERINE pregnancy (viable fetus, closed cervix). This patient has an EMPTY uterus at β-hCG 4,800 — far beyond the discriminating zone. The adnexal mass and free fluid confirm ectopic, not IUP.
- PID: PID can cause pelvic pain and cervical motion tenderness, but would be β-hCG NEGATIVE. This patient's positive β-hCG with empty uterus and adnexal mass indicates ectopic pregnancy, not infection.
- Wait for β-hCG doubling: Serial β-hCG is for STABLE patients with uncertain diagnosis. This patient has adnexal mass, free fluid, and hemodynamic instability — the diagnosis is clear (ruptured ectopic), and waiting for labs risks maternal death. Proceed to surgery.
- Methotrexate regardless of status: Methotrexate is CONTRAINDICATED in ruptured/unstable ectopic. It requires 7-14 days to work; a hemodynamically unstable patient needs SURGERY. Methotrexate is for STABLE, UNRUPTURED ectopics meeting specific criteria.

Reference: Ectopic Pregnancy - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539860/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 34 weeks gestation presents with headache, visual changes (seeing spots), and RUQ pain. She has 3+ proteinuria on dipstick. This is her first pregnancy.Vital SignsBP 168/112HR 88LabsPlatelets 92,000 (low)AST 186 U/L (elevated)ALT 202 U/L (elevated)Cr 1.2LDH 580 (elevated)Uric acid 8.2 (elevated) What syndrome does she have, and what is the management?

choices.text: Gestational hypertension — observe and recheck BP in 1 week
PREECLAMPSIA with SEVERE FEATURES and HELLP SYNDROME — this is an OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY:
Chronic hypertension with superimposed preeclampsia — continue home BP meds
Magnesium is only needed if seizures occur

explanation:
Correct Answer: Preeclampsia severity spectrum:
• Without severe features: BP ≥140/90, proteinuria, no end-organ damage
• With severe features: BP ≥160/110, platelets 1.1, liver enzymes >2x, RUQ pain, headache, visual changes, pulmonary edema
• HELLP: Hemolysis + Elevated Liver enzymes + Low Platelets
• Eclampsia: preeclampsia + seizures

Magnesium sulfate: PREVENTS seizures (eclampsia) in severe preeclampsia — give it BEFORE seizures occur, not after. Magnes trial: 58% reduction in eclampsia.

Delivery is the cure: only definitive treatment. Timing balances maternal risk (worsening disease) vs fetal risk (prematurity). ≥37 weeks: deliver. 34-37 weeks with severe features: consider delivery. <34 weeks: stabilize, steroids, deliver if worsening.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- BP 168/112 = severe (≥160/110)
- Headache + visual changes = neurologic severe features
- RUQ pain + elevated AST/ALT = liver involvement
- Platelets 92K + LDH 580 = HELLP syndrome
- 34 weeks = give betamethasone for fetal lungs
- Magnesium for seizure prophylaxis
- Antihypertensives to goal <160/110
- Plan delivery within 24-48h after steroids

Evidence: Preeclampsia - StatPearls - ACOG Practice Bulletin. StatPearls. NBK570611. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Gestational HTN — observe: This is NOT just gestational hypertension. She has SEVERE features (BP ≥160/110, neurologic symptoms, liver enzymes >2x, low platelets). This is preeclampsia with severe features/HELLP syndrome requiring admission, magnesium, and delivery planning.
- Chronic HTN superimposed: While superimposed preeclampsia is possible, the key here is recognizing the SEVERITY. Regardless of whether hypertension was pre-existing, she now has severe features (HELLP, neurologic symptoms) requiring emergent management, not continuing home medications.
- Acute fatty liver of pregnancy: AFLP can look similar (elevated LFTs, coagulopathy) but typically has hypoglycemia (from liver failure), more significant coagulopathy (low fibrinogen, elevated PT/INR), and normal or low platelets without the hemolysis pattern. HELLP has elevated LDH with hemolysis. Both require delivery.
- Magnesium only after seizures: This is WRONG. Magnesium is for PREVENTION of seizures in severe preeclampsia. The Magpie trial showed 58% reduction in eclampsia with prophylactic magnesium. Waiting for seizures to occur increases maternal and fetal morbidity.

Reference: Preeclampsia - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK570611/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 32-year-old G3P3 delivered vaginally 20 minutes ago after a prolonged second stage of labor with oxytocin augmentation. She is now having heavy vaginal bleeding with clots. The uterus is soft and boggy on palpation despite fundal massage. Estimated blood loss is 1,200 mL.Vital SignsBP 92/58HR 124ExamUterus Soft, boggy, above umbilicus despite massagePerineum Intact, no lacerationsPlacenta Delivered complete, membranes intact What is the most likely cause and treatment sequence?

choices.text: Retained placental fragments — manual exploration needed
UTERINE ATONY causing postpartum hemorrhage (PPH) — this is the most common cause
Observation — bleeding will slow on its own

explanation:
Correct Answer: 4 T's of PPH:
• Tone (70-80%): uterine atony — soft boggy uterus
• Trauma (20%): lacerations, hematoma, uterine rupture
• Tissue (10%): retained placenta or membranes
• Thrombin (Uterotonic agents:
• Oxytocin: first-line, 10-40 units IV
• Methylergonovine: avoid in HTN (causes vasoconstriction)
• Carboprost (15-methyl PGF2α): avoid in asthma (bronchospasm)
• Misoprostol: rectal 800-1000mcg, no contraindications

Tranexamic acid: WOMAN trial showed 30% reduction in death from bleeding when given within 3 hours. Give 1g IV early.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- EBL 1,200 mL = PPH (>500 vaginal, >1000 cesarean)
- Soft boggy uterus = atony (TONE)
- Placenta complete, no lacerations = not tissue or trauma
- Prolonged labor + oxytocin = atony risk factors
- First: bimanual massage
- Second: uterotonics (oxytocin, methylergonovine, carboprost, misoprostol)
- Tranexamic acid early
- Tamponade if medical management fails
- Hypotension + tachycardia = consider transfusion

Evidence: Postpartum Hemorrhage - StatPearls - ACOG Practice Bulletin. StatPearls. NBK499988. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Retained placenta: The placenta was delivered complete with intact membranes — retained tissue is unlikely. The key finding is the SOFT BOGGY UTERUS, which indicates ATONY, not retained products. If tissue were the cause, the uterus might still be firm but bleeding would continue.
- Cervical laceration: The perineum is intact and no lacerations are noted. Additionally, the soft boggy uterus points to atony as the cause. Cervical lacerations would present with continued bleeding despite a firm, contracted uterus. Inspection would reveal the laceration.
- DIC: DIC is rare as a PRIMARY cause of PPH (<1%). DIC more commonly develops SECONDARY to massive hemorrhage or other conditions (AFE, sepsis, placental abruption). The initial presentation here is atony. Labs for DIC would show low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, prolonged PT/PTT.
- Observation: This is DANGEROUS. PPH is a life-threatening emergency. EBL 1,200 mL with hemodynamic instability (BP 92/58, HR 124) requires immediate intervention, not observation. Delay in treatment is a major contributor to maternal mortality from PPH.

Reference: PPH - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499988/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 34 weeks presents with new-onset headache, visual changes (scotomata), and RUQ pain. She had normal blood pressures at all prior visits.Vital SignsBP 168/108HR 88LabsPlatelets 88,000 (low)AST 286 (elevated)ALT 312 (elevated)Cr 1.4 (elevated)LDH 680 (elevated)Urine_protein 3+ on dipstick What syndrome does this represent, and what is the definitive treatment?

choices.text: Gestational hypertension — start antihypertensives and continue pregnancy to term
Preeclampsia with severe features / HELLP syndrome —
Chronic hypertension with superimposed pregnancy changes
Acute fatty liver of pregnancy — supportive care only

explanation:
Correct Answer: HELLP syndrome: Hemolysis (elevated LDH, schistocytes), Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets. This is a severe variant of preeclampsia. Preeclampsia with severe features criteria: BP ≥160/110, platelets 2x normal, creatinine >1.1, pulmonary edema, visual/cerebral symptoms. Management: (1) IV magnesium sulfate (seizure prophylaxis — MOST IMPORTANT), (2) antihypertensives for acute BP control (IV labetalol or IV hydralazine — target <160/110), (3) betamethasone if <37 weeks (fetal lung maturity), (4) DELIVERY is the only definitive treatment. At 34 weeks with severe features: deliver after stabilization. Eclampsia = seizures in preeclampsia — treat with more magnesium, NOT traditional anticonvulsants.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 34 weeks, new-onset hypertension = preeclampsia spectrum
- BP 168/108 = severe range (≥160/110)
- Headache + visual changes = cerebral involvement (severe feature)
- RUQ pain = liver capsule distension
- Platelets 88K, AST/ALT elevated, LDH elevated = HELLP
- Proteinuria 3+ confirms preeclampsia
- Multiple severe features → magnesium + stabilize + deliver

Evidence: Preeclampsia - StatPearls - ACOG Practice Bulletin 222. 2020.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Gestational hypertension — continue pregnancy: This is NOT simple gestational hypertension (which lacks proteinuria and severe features). This patient has proteinuria, severe hypertension, headache, visual changes, thrombocytopenia, and elevated LFTs — multiple severe features requiring delivery, not expectant management.
- Chronic hypertension: Chronic hypertension is diagnosed BEFORE 20 weeks or pre-pregnancy. This patient had normal BPs at all prior visits with new-onset hypertension at 34 weeks, which is preeclampsia. The thrombocytopenia and elevated LFTs confirm severe disease.
- Acute fatty liver: AFLP shares some features (elevated LFTs, can have low platelets) but classically presents with hypoglycemia, markedly elevated bilirubin, and DIC. AFLP is rarer and the hypertension/proteinuria pattern here is more consistent with preeclampsia/HELLP.
- TTP: TTP causes thrombocytopenia and hemolysis (microangiopathic) but with NORMAL liver enzymes, prominent neurologic symptoms, and fever. The elevated liver enzymes, proteinuria, and pregnancy context point to HELLP syndrome, not TTP.

Reference: Preeclampsia - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK570611/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 29-year-old woman with a positive urine pregnancy test presents with 6 weeks of amenorrhea, left lower quadrant pain, and vaginal spotting. She has a history of PID and left tubal surgery.Vital SignsBP 110/72HR 96LabsbHCG 2,800 mIU/mLImagingTransvaginal_US Empty uterus, left adnexal mass 2.8 cm with 'ring of fire' Doppler pattern, small amount of free fluid in cul-de-sac What finding confirms the location, and what are the treatment options?

choices.text: Threatened miscarriage — expectant management with serial bHCG
Ectopic pregnancy — treatment: methotrexate if hemodynamically stable, unruptured, and bHCG <5000;
Corpus luteum cyst — normal finding in early pregnancy
Complete miscarriage — no further treatment needed
Normal intrauterine pregnancy too early to visualize

explanation:
Correct Answer: Ectopic pregnancy: implantation outside the uterus (95% tubal). 'Discriminatory zone': at bHCG >1500-2000 mIU/mL, a normal IUP should be visible on transvaginal US. Empty uterus + bHCG above discriminatory zone = ectopic until proven otherwise. Adnexal mass with ring of fire (increased vascularity on Doppler) confirms ectopic location. Risk factors: prior PID (strongest), tubal surgery, prior ectopic, IUD, smoking. Methotrexate criteria: hemodynamically stable, unruptured, bHCG <5000 (some use <10,000), no fetal cardiac activity, reliable follow-up. Surgical: if ruptured, hemodynamically unstable, failed methotrexate, or contraindication to methotrexate.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 6 weeks amenorrhea + positive pregnancy test + LLQ pain = ectopic suspected
- bHCG 2800 = above discriminatory zone (>1500-2000)
- Empty uterus at this bHCG level = NOT normal IUP
- Left adnexal mass with ring of fire = ectopic confirmed
- History of PID and tubal surgery = major risk factors
- Hemodynamically stable + unruptured + bHCG <5000 = methotrexate candidate
- Free fluid = small amount (not massive hemoperitoneum)

Evidence: Ectopic Pregnancy - StatPearls - ACOG Practice Bulletin 193. 2018.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Threatened miscarriage: In threatened miscarriage, the pregnancy is IN the uterus (visible gestational sac). This patient has an EMPTY uterus with bHCG above the discriminatory zone and an adnexal mass — this is ectopic, not miscarriage.
- Corpus luteum cyst: Corpus luteum cysts are normal in early pregnancy and can appear as adnexal masses, but the 'ring of fire' pattern with an EMPTY uterus at bHCG 2800 makes ectopic the diagnosis. A normal corpus luteum would have a concurrent IUP visible.
- Complete miscarriage: Complete miscarriage would show an empty uterus with a DECLINING bHCG. This patient has bHCG of 2800 (still rising) with an adnexal mass — the pregnancy is ectopic, not passed.
- Too early to see IUP: At bHCG >1500-2000, a normal IUP should be visible on TVUS (discriminatory zone). This patient's bHCG is 2800 with an empty uterus — this exceeds the discriminatory zone and indicates ectopic pregnancy.

Reference: Ectopic Pregnancy - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539860/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 32-year-old G3P2 with gestational diabetes and estimated fetal weight 4,500g is in the second stage of labor. The fetal head delivers but retracts against the perineum ("turtle sign"). Gentle downward traction on the head does not deliver the anterior shoulder. What is the maneuver sequence for this obstetric emergency?

choices.text: Apply fundal pressure to push the baby out
McRoberts maneuver (hyperflexion of maternal hips) with suprapubic pressure —
Continue aggressive downward traction on the fetal head

explanation:
Correct Answer: Shoulder dystocia: anterior shoulder impacted behind pubic symphysis. 'Turtle sign' = head delivers then retracts. HELPERR mnemonic: (H) call for Help, (E) Evaluate for Episiotomy, (L) Legs — McROBERTS MANEUVER (hyperflex maternal thighs against abdomen — widens pelvic outlet, first-line, resolves 40-50%), (P) suprapubic Pressure (NOT fundal — push anterior shoulder under symphysis), (E) Enter — internal rotation maneuvers (Rubin II: push posterior aspect of anterior shoulder toward fetal chest; Woods corkscrew: push anterior aspect of posterior shoulder 180°), (R) Remove posterior arm, (R) Roll patient to all-fours (Gaskin maneuver). NEVER apply fundal pressure (worsens impaction) or excessive lateral traction (Erb palsy — brachial plexus injury C5-C6).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Fetal head delivers but retracts = turtle sign = shoulder dystocia
- Macrosomia (4500g) + GDM = risk factors
- Gentle traction failed = need maneuvers
- McRoberts FIRST: hyperflex hips → straightens sacrum, widens outlet
- Add suprapubic pressure (NOT fundal)
- If fails: internal rotation maneuvers
- NEVER fundal pressure (worsens impaction)

Evidence: Shoulder Dystocia - StatPearls - ACOG Practice Bulletin 178. 2017.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Fundal pressure: Fundal pressure is CONTRAINDICATED in shoulder dystocia — it pushes the anterior shoulder further INTO the pubic symphysis, worsening impaction and increasing injury risk. SUPRAPUBIC pressure (pushing anteriorly on the anterior shoulder) is the correct adjunct.
- Immediate cesarean: C-section is not feasible once the head has delivered. The Zavanelli maneuver (pushing the head back in and performing emergency C-section) is a LAST RESORT after all other maneuvers have failed. McRoberts resolves most cases.
- Aggressive downward traction: Excessive lateral traction on the fetal head causes brachial plexus injury (Erb palsy — C5-C6: waiter's tip position). Maneuvers that change pelvic geometry (McRoberts) or rotate the shoulders (Rubin/Woods) are the correct approach.
- Kristeller maneuver: The Kristeller maneuver (fundal pressure during delivery) is controversial even in normal deliveries and is CONTRAINDICATED in shoulder dystocia for the same reason as any fundal pressure — it worsens anterior shoulder impaction.

Reference: Shoulder Dystocia - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470427/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 34-year-old G3P2 at 32 weeks gestation presents with sudden painless bright red vaginal bleeding. She has no contractions and no abdominal tenderness. explanation:

explanation:

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 40 weeks with gestational diabetes delivers the fetal head, but the anterior shoulder does not deliver with gentle downward traction. There is a "turtle sign" as the fetal head retracts against the perineum.Vital SignsBP 128/82HR 90RR 18Temp 98.6°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Shoulder dystocia is diagnosed when the fetal head delivers but the anterior shoulder is impacted behind the pubic symphysis. The 'turtle sign' (retraction of the head against the perineum) is the hallmark clinical finding.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Turtle sign = fetal head retracts → anterior shoulder impacted behind symphysis
- Risk factors: macrosomia, GDM, prior shoulder dystocia, operative vaginal delivery
- Obstetric emergency — must be resolved within minutes to prevent brachial plexus injury
- McRoberts maneuver + suprapubic pressure are first-line interventions

Evidence: Shoulder Dystocia - StatPearls - Gherman RB, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK470427. Updated 2024.

- Cord prolapse: Cord prolapse occurs before delivery of the head and presents differently.
- Uterine rupture: Presents with fetal bradycardia, maternal pain, and loss of station — head has already delivered here.
- CPD requiring cesarean: Head has delivered; this is shoulder impaction, not CPD.

stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 at 34 weeks presents with new-onset headache, visual changes, and BP 158/102 mmHg confirmed on two readings. She has 2+ proteinuria on dipstick.Vital SignsBP 158/102HR 90RR 18Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 99% Which workup is most appropriate to assess disease severity?

choices.text: CBC with platelets, LFTs, creatinine, LDH, and uric acid

explanation:
Correct Answer: Preeclampsia evaluation requires labs to assess for severe features and HELLP syndrome: CBC with platelets, LFTs (AST/ALT), creatinine, LDH, and uric acid. These identify thrombocytopenia, hepatic dysfunction, and hemolysis.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- ACOG diagnostic criteria: HTN ≥140/90 after 20 weeks + proteinuria OR end-organ dysfunction
- Severe features: platelets 2x ULN, creatinine >1.1, headache/visual changes
- HELLP: Hemolysis (LDH ↑, schistocytes), Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
- Uric acid correlates with disease severity but not diagnostic

Evidence: Preeclampsia - StatPearls - Rana S, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK570611. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- 24-hour urine only: Protein/creatinine ratio has replaced 24h collection; also doesn't assess severe features.
- CT head: Not routinely indicated; reserved for suspected intracranial pathology beyond typical preeclampsia.
- Antiphospholipid antibodies: For recurrent pregnancy loss workup, not acute preeclampsia evaluation.
- Renal ultrasound: Not part of standard preeclampsia workup.

Reference: Preeclampsia - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK570611/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 36-year-old G2P1 presents for routine prenatal visit at 36 weeks. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. She asks about testing for Group B Streptococcus.Vital SignsBP 118/72HR 78RR 14Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the recommended approach for GBS screening?

choices.text: Urine culture for GBS at 36 weeks
Blood culture for GBS at 38 weeks
PCR testing for GBS at onset of labor only
No screening needed if previous pregnancy was GBS-negative

explanation:
Correct Answer: Universal GBS rectovaginal culture at 36-37 weeks is recommended (updated CDC/ACOG guidelines). Positive results guide intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis to prevent early-onset neonatal GBS disease.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Rectovaginal swab at 36-37 weeks (updated from 35-37 in 2020 CDC guidelines)
- GBS-positive: intrapartum penicillin G or ampicillin ≥4 hours before delivery
- GBS bacteriuria at any time = GBS-positive for this pregnancy → no additional culture needed
- Prior GBS-negative pregnancy does NOT predict current colonization — rescreen each pregnancy

Evidence: GBS in Pregnancy - StatPearls - Puopolo KM, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK482500. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Urine culture for GBS: GBS bacteriuria is noted during routine urine culture but is not the screening method — rectovaginal swab is standard.
- Blood culture: GBS is not routinely detected in blood in asymptomatic colonized women.
- PCR at labor only: Rapid PCR exists but universal culture at 36-37 weeks remains the standard screening approach.
- Skip if prior negative: GBS colonization is transient; must rescreen each pregnancy.

Reference: GBS in Pregnancy - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482500/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 26 weeks presents for routine prenatal care. She has no significant medical history. BMI is 27. She passed her 1-hour glucose challenge test at 24 weeks with a value of 128 mg/dL (threshold 140). She asks if she needs further diabetes testing.Vital SignsBP 116/72HR 74RR 14Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most appropriate response regarding glucose screening?

choices.text: No further testing needed — she passed the screening test
Proceed directly to 3-hour glucose tolerance test

explanation:
Correct Answer: A 1-hour glucose challenge test (GCT) result negative screen that does not require further testing. The two-step approach uses 50g GCT as screening followed by 100g 3-hour GTT only if GCT is ≥140.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Two-step screening: 50g GCT at 24-28 weeks → if ≥140 → 100g 3-hour GTT
- GCT <140 mg/dL = negative screen, no further testing needed
- GDM diagnosis (Carpenter-Coustan): ≥2 abnormal GTT values (fasting ≥95, 1h ≥180, 2h ≥155, 3h ≥140)
- Risk factors for early screening (<24 wk): BMI ≥25, prior GDM, family history, PCOS

Evidence: Gestational Diabetes - StatPearls - McIntyre HD, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK545196. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Proceed to 3-hour GTT: Only indicated if GCT is ≥140 mg/dL; her value of 128 is below threshold.
- Repeat at 32 weeks: Not standard practice; repeat screening only if new risk factors develop.
- Home glucose monitoring: Not indicated in patient who screened negative.
- HbA1c: A1c is not the recommended GDM screening test; less sensitive in pregnancy.

Reference: Gestational Diabetes - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK545196/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 7 weeks by LMP presents with vaginal spotting and mild cramping. No passage of tissue. Cervical os is closed on speculum exam. She has no prior ultrasound in this pregnancy.Vital SignsBP 112/68HR 80RR 14Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most appropriate initial workup?

choices.text: Expectant management with follow-up in 1 week
Quantitative β-hCG, Rh status, and transvaginal ultrasound

explanation:
Correct Answer: First-trimester bleeding with closed os (threatened abortion) requires quantitative β-hCG, Rh status (for RhoGAM if Rh-negative), and transvaginal ultrasound to determine viability, location, and gestational age.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- TV-US determines: intrauterine vs ectopic, viability (cardiac activity), gestational age
- β-hCG >2,000-3,000 should show IUP on TV-US (discriminatory zone)
- Rh-negative patients need RhoGAM to prevent alloimmunization
- Threatened abortion (closed os, bleeding) has 50% chance of continuing to viable pregnancy

Evidence: First Trimester Bleeding - StatPearls - Hendriks E, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK541120. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Expectant management: Cannot rule out ectopic pregnancy without imaging; must establish pregnancy location.
- CT abdomen: Radiation exposure inappropriate in pregnancy; TV-US is the imaging modality of choice.
- Diagnostic laparoscopy: Invasive and premature; US and β-hCG can establish diagnosis non-invasively.
- Progesterone only: Progesterone has limited utility; β-hCG and US provide more actionable information.

Reference: First Trimester Bleeding - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK541120/ [Accessed 2025]
